note:
将这本书送到我手中的后勤工作，以及付出的人力代价，令人难以置信，但我们已经有点习惯了。
新冠肺炎的停工事件突显了供应链动态的重要性和复杂性。在一个大流行塑造的世界里，我们对电子商务的依赖已经爆炸式增长，从个人防护用品到卫生纸，再到冷冰冰的平装书奥秘，我们对电子商务的依赖无处不在。Adobe最近的一份报告发现，与去年同期相比，在线消费总额增长了77%，增长速度加快了“四到六年”。这种增长有非常真实的人力成本，而作为一个社会，我们没有充分考虑到这一点，也没有采取足够的行动。
虽然人们认可他们可以看到的一线工人的贡献，如医生和护士、邮递员和杂货店工人，但物流工人的整个隐藏基础设施使在线经济保持繁荣。这些工人也在前线，但他们在幕后。大多数人挣的是最低工资，工作时间长，精疲力竭，压力大，没有强有力的保护措施，以防生病或受伤。事实是，许多公司并没有把保护这些工人作为优先事项。在新冠肺炎之前是这样，但疫情让这个问题重新变得紧迫，促使亚马逊、沃尔玛、塔吉特和联邦快递等公司的工人组织罢工。随着失业率达到前所未有的水平，越来越多的人将在物流业找到工作。
这个劳动节，是时候思考企业如何更好地支持和保护这一重要但经常被遗忘的劳动力群体。
想象一下，在一家主要制造商，有一位名叫杰克的包裹处理员，他的轮班时间是把沉重的箱子抬到传送带上。这是一个艰巨的动作，杰克要重复几千次才能打卡。作为一名有10年工作经验的老兵，杰克在同一个仓库地板上执行这项特殊任务的次数已经数不清了。在这个特殊的夜晚，他在和孩子们玩到很晚后很累，他把一张碟子滑到了他的背上。不幸的是，对于今天数以百万计的工人来说，杰克的困境往往是现实。
根据美国劳工统计局(Bureau Of Labor Statistics)的数据，美国每年有5%的仓库工人在工作中受伤-高于全国平均水平。排在服务人员之后，与消防员和警察一样，运输/航运和制造/生产分别位居第二和第三位，是因工伤导致休工天数最多的职业。涉及繁重的搬运、艰苦的重复和操作复杂的机械的工作都有严重的风险。
伤害可能会对工人的身体和经济造成毁灭性的影响。请假不仅会导致工资损失，还会让人们因为健康相关的费用而负债累累，造成难以爬出的健康贫困陷阱。对雇主来说，工伤也是代价高昂的。Liberty Mutual的一项研究利用美国劳工统计局(U.S.Bureau Of Labor Statistics)和美国国家社会保险学院(National Academy Of Social Insurance)的数据发现，在运输和仓储行业，严重的非致命伤害每周造成的损失为8404万美元。将工作场所安全放在首位符合企业的最佳利益。
其中一个挑战是，工作场所安全的传统方法速度慢、不准确且成本高昂。在没有实际干预的情况下，组织每年在每个工人身上花费的工伤预防费用估计在2000美元以上。在制造业和物流业中，每名全职员工每年额外支付2,000多美元的工伤补偿。目前，没有标准的解决方案来防止工作场所伤害，同时降低成本，使得像杰克这样的工人得不到足够的保护。幸运的是，利用技术创新(包括传感器和可穿戴设备)的数字平台和工具正在推动防止工作场所事故和伤害的新方法。
以Fruish投资组合中的一家公司Strongarm为例。StrongARM搭建了集新一代工业可穿戴设备、大数据分析和智能算法于一体的技术平台。它旨在为工人、雇主和工伤赔偿保险公司实现行业动态的现代化。该公司的符合GDPR的可穿戴硬件设备和名为FUSE的数据平台提供实时伤害预防反馈并收集数据，以支持精确的干预措施，以全面减少伤害，并已将客户的伤害发生率同比降低了40%以上。
StrongARM还在其FUSE平台上推出了一套新功能，包括CDC通信、接近警报(即，向彼此相距6英尺以内的工作人员发出通知)和暴露分析(了解谁在什么时间、多长时间与谁进行了互动，准确暴露任何潜在的接触转移)，从而帮助确保了员工在大流行期间的安全。这些增强的能力可以让员工更快地重返工作岗位，赚取急需的收入，同时将新冠肺炎的风险降低95%。
Fetch Robotics是另一家使用技术创新和数字平台来促进工人安全的公司。FETCH制造了一种自主移动机器人(AMR)，它可以在仓库、工厂和配送中心内运输材料，同时还可以收集环境数据。这可以减轻人类工人的繁重搬运负担，并确保工作环境中的条件(如高温)保持安全。2020年6月，该公司宣布将推出一款消毒AMR，可以在1.5小时内对10万平方英尺以上的空间进行净化，帮助工人在病毒传播的情况下保持安全，更快地恢复工作。
在题为“新冠肺炎对技术创新的影响”的报告中，莱克斯研究公司发现，新冠肺炎的爆发可能会推动拥有主要制造和物流业务的公司评估机器人的潜力。更多的公司将探索如何实现流程自动化，特别是那些可重复和可预测的流程。这样的发现不可避免地引发了这样的问题：自动化程度的提高将如何影响工人--永恒的“机器人会抢走所有的工作吗？”问题。然而，我们距离人类工人被淘汰的世界还有很长的路要走(问问埃隆·马斯克就知道了)。
例如，机器人仍然不擅长捡起小的或形状奇特的物体。在可预见的未来，企业将依赖于物流工人，并有责任保护这些工人的安全。在工厂或仓库地面上贴上要求的OSHA标志是不够的。企业需要做得更多。幸运的是，在这种情况下，做正确的事情就是做好事。通过拥抱技术创新，促进工人安全是双赢的。
note_en:
The logistics of getting the book to me, and the human toll it takes, are mind boggling, but we have become somewhat inured to them.
 COVID-19 lockdowns have put a spotlight on the importance and complexity of supply chain dynamics. In a world shaped by the pandemic, our reliance on e-commerce for everything from PPE to toilet paper to hard-boiled paperback mysteries has exploded. A  recent report from Adobe found that total online spending is up 77% year-over-year, accelerating growth by “four to six years.” That growth has a very real human cost, and one that we don’t think about or act on enough as a society.
  While people recognize the contributions of frontline workers they can see like doctors and nurses, postal carriers and grocery store workers, there’s an entire hidden infrastructure of logistics workers that keeps the online economy humming. These workers are also on the frontlines, but they are behind the scenes. Most earn minimum wage and work long, grueling, high-stress shifts without strong protections in the event they get sick or injured. The fact is that many corporations haven’t made protections for those workers a priority. That was true before COVID-19, but the pandemic gave the issue a renewed urgency,  prompting workers from Amazon, Walmart, Target and FedEx, among others, to organize walkouts. And with unprecedented levels of unemployment, more and more people are going to find jobs in the logistics sector.
 This  Labor   Day, it’s time to think about how corporations can better support and protect this vital but often forgotten segment of the workforce.
  Imagine there’s a package handler at a major manufacturer named Jack who spends his shifts heaving heavy boxes onto a conveyor belt. It’s an arduous movement that Jack will repeat a few thousand times before he punches out. As a 10-year veteran on the job, Jack has performed this singular task on this same warehouse floor more times than he can count. On this particular night, he’s tired after staying up late playing with his kids, and he slips a disk in his back. Unfortunately, Jack’s plight is all too often a reality for millions of workers today.
 According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, 5% of warehouse workers in the U.S. experience an injury on the job each year—higher than the national average. After service workers, like firefighters and police, transportation/shipping and manufacturing/production rank second and third as  the occupations with the largest number of workplace injuries resulting in days away from work. Jobs that involve heavy lifting, arduous repetition and operating complex machinery come with serious risk.
 Injuries can be devastating for workers, both physically and financially. Taking time off work can not only result in lost wages, but also drive people into debt  due to health-related expenses, creating health-poverty traps that are difficult to climb out of. Worker injuries are also costly for employers.  A study from Liberty Mutual, using data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics and the National Academy of Social Insurance, found that serious, nonfatal injuries cost $84.04 million a week in the transportation and warehousing industry. It is in corporations’ best interest to prioritize workplace safety.
 One challenge is that traditional approaches to workplace safety are slow, inaccurate and costly. Without practical interventions, organizations spend an estimated $2,000+ per worker annually on injury prevention. Within manufacturing and logistics industries, it costs an additional $2,000+ annually for workers’ compensation per full-time employee. Currently, there is no standard solution to preventing workplace injuries while lowering costs, leaving workers like Jack without adequate protections. Fortunately, digital platforms and tools that leverage technological innovation, including sensors and wearables, are advancing new ways to prevent workplace accidents and injuries.
 Take for example  StrongArm, one of Flourish’s portfolio companies. StrongArm has built a technology platform that integrates a new generation of industrial wearables, big data analytics and smart algorithms. It is designed to modernize industry dynamics for workers, employers and workers’ compensation insurers. The company’s GDPR-compliant wearable hardware devices and data platform called FUSE deliver real-time injury prevention feedback and collect data to support precise interventions for overall injury reduction and has reduced injury rates by more than 40% year-over-year for its clients.
 StrongArm has also helped keep workers safe during the pandemic by launching a new suite of capabilities on its FUSE platform, including CDC communication, proximity alerts (i.e., notifications to workers within six feet of one another), and exposure analysis (understanding who has interacted with whom, at what time, and for what duration, exposing any potential contact transfer with accuracy). These enhanced capabilities can get workers back to work faster, earning vitally needed income while reducing COVID-19 risk by 95%.
 Fetch Robotics is another company using technological innovation and digital platforms to promote worker safety. Fetch makes an Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR) that can transport materials within warehouses, factories and distribution centers while also gathering environmental data. This can relieve the burden of heavy lifting from human workers and ensure that conditions, like heat, remain safe in work environments. In June 2020, the company announced that it was launching a disinfecting AMR that can  decontaminate spaces larger than 100,000 square feet in 1.5 hours, helping workers stay safe and get back to work quicker amid the spread of the virus.
  In its report titled,  “The Impact of COVID-19 on Tech Innovation,” Lux Research found that the outbreak of COVID-19 will likely push corporations with major manufacturing and logistics operations to assess the potential of robotics. More companies will explore how they can automate processes, particularly those that are repeatable and predictable. Findings like these inevitably lead to questions about how increased automation will impact workers — the eternal “will robots take all the jobs?” question. However, we are still a long way away from a world where human workers are obsolete ( just ask Elon Musk).
 Robots are still not good at picking up  small or oddly shaped objects, for instance. For the foreseeable future, corporations will depend on logistics workers and have a responsibility to protect the safety of those workers. It’s not enough to plaster the required OSHA sign on the factory or warehouse floor. Corporations need to do more. Fortunately in this case, the right thing to do is the good thing to do. By embracing technological innovation, promoting worker safety is a win-win.
title: 在这个劳动节，请为那些让你上门送货成为可能的工人们想想吧
title_en: This Labor Day, spare a thought for the workers who made your doorstep delivery possible

巴黎(CNN)根据Facebook的一份声明，Facebook阻止了一名患有不治之症的法国男子在社交媒体网站上流传自己的死亡。
57岁的阿兰·科克(Alain Cocq)来自法国东部的第戎，他患有一种罕见的不治之症，导致他的动脉粘连在一起。
他估计，在停止所有药物、食物和饮料后，他只有几天的生命了，他计划在周五晚上这样做。
科克原本打算在这个平台上播放他临终的日子，以提高人们对法国关于协助死亡的法律的认识。
在周六的一份声明中，Facebook表示，直播被阻止，以避免宣传自我伤害。
该公司在声明中表示：我们的心与阿兰·科克(Alain Cocq)联系在一起，感谢他在这一令人悲伤的情况下所经历的一切，以及每一位个人受到影响的人。
虽然我们尊重阿兰提请人们注意这一重要问题的决定，但根据专家的建议，我们正在阻止在他的账户上进行直播，因为对自杀企图的描述可能会引发并促进更多的自我伤害。
科克之前曾游说总统伊曼纽尔·马克龙允许他在积极的医疗救助下死亡，但没有成功。
安乐死在法国是非法的。法国法律还规定，深度持续镇静是不合法的，这种镇静可以加速一个人的死亡，使他们直到死亡时失去知觉，除非在2016年克莱伊-莱昂内蒂法律(Claeys-Leonetti Law)规定的特定情况下，该法律也要求一个人的死亡即将到来。但法国公民确实有权停止医疗护理，根据法国法律，自杀不会受到起诉。
科克被限制在轮椅上，并成立了一个改善残疾人生活的组织。7月20日，他给马克龙写了一封信，请求总统允许他有尊严地死去，描述了他遭受的极端暴力的苦难。
我想向你们表明，在这一天，我发现自己处于一种精神错乱、身体机能失调、因痛苦而残废的境地，他在这封信中写道，CNN已经看到了这封信。他在信中写道，这一天，我发现自己处于一种精神错乱、因痛苦而跛脚的境地。CNN已经看到了这封信。
总统先生，你能忍受把肠子倒进袋子里，把膀胱倒进袋子里，用袋子喂你吗，必须有第三方给你洗澡，才能让你因难以忍受的疼痛而跛足吗？&#34；科克在信中写道。
科克敦促马克龙审查法国法律，这些法律防止卫生保健专业人员用药物加速垂死患者的死亡。
他写道：我只是要求有尊严地离开，并得到积极的医疗帮助，因为我功能失调的身体阻止了我在家人和朋友的陪伴下这样做。
他写道，有些人使用积极安乐死或协助自杀这个词，但对我来说，最合适的词是在积极的医疗帮助下有尊严地结束生命。
马克龙总统在回信中表示，他被科克的信所感动，并钦佩他在与疾病进行无休止的斗争中所表现出的非凡意志力。
然而，他说他不能遵从这一要求，因为他不能凌驾于法律之上，也不能要求任何人超越我们目前的法律框架。
在周五接受美国有线电视新闻网采访时，科克表示，他预计马克龙不会满足他的要求，但他感谢他在信中表现出的同情心。
今天，我充满了充实。我决定在6月26日结束我的生命，也就是我让我的医疗助理把我后来寄给总统的信打出来的那一天。
这是一个残酷的决定，但我可以告诉你，我已经很久没有感觉这么好了，他告诉CNN。
他说：我知道我将要忍受我以前从未忍受过的痛苦，大约5到7天。
对于试图通过视频直播他的死亡一事，他在周五表示：我这么做不是为了偷窥。我想告诉人们一些我们都知道但拒绝谈论的事情。痛苦。&#34；
他说：我选择表现出这种痛苦。民主的基础是公民有自由的选择。死亡应该是民主的。
科克还说，作为一名基督徒，他不相信自己的所作所为是反宗教的。
目前还不清楚任何协助他的人是否会被起诉，也不清楚当局是否计划进行干预。CNN已经联系爱丽舍宫和卫生部请其置评。
索菲·梅杰伯格，Handi Mais Pas que！(不仅仅是残疾人)的副总裁，是可可的朋友。
她告诉CNN，她相信法国人已经准备好就安乐死展开辩论。
他大约在两年前开始谈论这件事。我花了时间去理解它，接受它。我也病了，因为我自己也患有多发性硬化症，她说。
我昨天哭了，但是阿兰让我高兴起来的。梅德杰伯格说，他对自己的倡议非常平静。
在接受CNN采访时，法国有尊严死亡权利协会(ADMD)的执行董事菲利普·洛埃克批评法国帮助垂死的法律，声称他们没有考虑到那些生活成了噩梦的病人，他们的生活已经变成了生存。
他说，法律需要修改，这样人们才能选择自己的死亡方式。
note_en:
Paris (CNN)Facebook has prevented a French man with an incurable illness from streaming his own death on the social media site, according to a company statement.
 Alain Cocq, 57, from Dijon in eastern France, has a rare incurable medical condition that causes his arteries to stick together.
 He estimates he will only have days to live after stopping all medication, food and drink, which he planned to do on Friday evening.
   Cocq had intended to broadcast his dying days on the platform, to raise awareness about France's laws on assisted dying.
 In a statement Saturday Facebook said the live stream was prevented to avoid promoting self-harm.
  "Our hearts go out to Alain Cocq for what he's going through in this sad situation and everyone who is personally affected by it," the company said in the statement.
 "While we respect Alain's decision to draw attention to this important issue, we are preventing live broadcasts on his account based on the advice of experts that the depiction of suicide attempts could be triggering and promote more self-harm."
 Cocq previously lobbied President Emmanuel Macron to allow him to die by "active medical assistance" but was unsuccessful.
 Euthanasia is illegal in France. French law also dictates that deep and continuous sedation, which can hasten a person's death and render them unconscious until they die, is not legal unless  under specific circumstances set out by the 2016 Claeys-Leonetti Law, which also requires a person's death to be imminent. But French citizens do have the right to stop medical care, and under French law there is no prosecution for suicide.
  Cocq, who is confined to a wheelchair and  founded an organization to improve the lives of disabled people, wrote a letter to Macron dated July 20 asking the President to allow him to die "with dignity," describing his "extremely violent suffering."
 "I would like to make it clear to you that on this day I find myself in a situation of having on sound mind, confined in a dysfunctional body, crippled by suffering," he wrote in the letter, which has been seen by CNN.
 "Would you withstand, Mr President, having your intestines emptied into a pouch, having your bladder emptied into a pouch and that you are fed by a pouch, that a third party must bathe you, to be crippled by unbearable pain?" Cocq wrote in the letter.
 Cocq urged Macron to review French laws that prevent health care professionals from hastening the deaths of their dying patients with medicine.
 "I simply ask to leave with dignity, with active medical assistance, because my dysfunctional body prevents me from doing so surrounded by my family and my friends," he wrote.
 "Some use the term 'active euthanasia' or 'assisted suicide,' but for me the term most suitable is 'end of life with dignity with active medical assistance,'" he wrote.
 Writing in response, President Macron said  he was "moved" by Cocq's letter and admired the "remarkable willpower" he had shown in fighting "incessant battles" with the illness.
 However, he said he could not comply with the request because he is "not situated above the law" and could not ask someone "to overstep our present legal framework."
  In an interview with CNN on Friday, Cocq said that he expected Macron would not be able to comply with his request but that he appreciated the "compassion" he had shown him in his letter.
 "Today, I am full of plenitude. I took the decision to end my life on June 26, which is when I asked my medical assistant to type the letter I later sent to the President."
 "It was a brutal decision, but I can tell you I haven't felt that good in a long time," he told CNN.
 "I know I am about to endure pain like I've never endured before, for about five to seven days," he said.
 Of the attempt to live-stream his death via video he said on Friday: "I am not doing this for voyeurism. I want to inform people about something we all know but refuse to talk about. Pain."
 "I chose to show this pain," he said. "The basis of a democracy is that citizens have a free choice. Death should be democratic."
 Cocq also said that, as a Christian, he did not believe what he was doing was anti-religious.
  It's unclear whether anyone assisting him would be prosecuted or whether authorities are planning to intervene. CNN has reached out to the Elysee and the health ministry for comment.
 Sophie Medjeberg, vice president of "Handi mais pas que!" (More than just disabled), is a friend of Cocq.
 She told CNN she believed French people were "ready" for a debate about assisted dying.
 "He started talking about it about two years ago. It took me time to understand it, to accept it. I am also ill, as I am myself suffering from multiple sclerosis," she said.
 "I cried yesterday, but Alain is the one who cheered me up. He is very serene in his initiative," Medjeberg said.
   Speaking to CNN, Philippe Lohéac, executive director of the French association for the right to die with dignity (ADMD), criticized France's assisting dying laws, claiming they do not "take into consideration sick people whose life has become a nightmare, whose life has become nothing but survival."
 He said the law needed to be changed so people could choose how they died.
title: Facebook阻止法国人流媒体他临终的日子
title_en: Facebook Stops French Man From Streaming His Dying Days

note:
地下城，所有角色扮演游戏的鼻祖，从来没有像今天这样受欢迎。1976年发布的这款桌面奇幻游戏在引入用户生成的故事方面是独一无二的。一个被称为“地下城大师”的人准备了一个故事，并向一群在幻想中扮演角色的玩家讲述了一个故事。就像一本“选择你自己的冒险”的书一样，故事是基于群体的集体选择展开的。在发布时，“D&amp；D”是一款相对小众的游戏，主要由狂热的角色扮演爱好者喜爱。
今天，全球有超过4000万人在玩研发，销售额连续五年以两位数的百分比增长。研发不再是一种边缘爱好，它在主流和名人文化中占据着突出的地位(称之为“陌生事物”的颠簸)。
D&amp；D的增长说明了一种更大的趋势。桌面游戏-一种典型的模拟体验，包括棋盘游戏、纸牌游戏和室内游戏-正被数字工具戏剧性地改进。虽然桌面游戏现代化的第一次尝试只是在数字领域复制游戏，但下一代游戏更进一步，集成了直播、用户生成内容(UGC)、音频产品和社区平台等工具。这种数字化转型正在重塑我们通过桌面游戏学习、玩耍和相互联系的方式。
因此，现代桌面游戏有可能比以往任何时候都更大。棋类游戏正在复兴-2018年整体市场达到120亿美元，并以9%的复合年增长率增长。像狼人和黑手党这样的室内游戏，玩家之间相互表演和欺骗，已经成长为全球热门游戏。中国的狼人杀APP在推出一年内就达到了超过7000万用户。即使是最古老的桌面游戏之一国际象棋，也正在强势卷土重来：今天，全球有6.05亿人下棋，国际象棋官方网站的流量在今年上半年大约翻了一番，达到每月9400万人次。
下一代桌面游戏将在四个关键方面与传统有所不同：直播、用户生成的内容、音频优先体验和在线社区平台。
直播-在Twitch、YouTube和Caffeine等平台上播放实时视频-已经达到了新的受欢迎程度，仅今年第二季度就有超过75亿小时的观看时间。但是，虽然媒体作为一种娱乐方式已经被广泛采用，但它也是一种非常有效的教学复杂活动的手段。
以国际象棋为例，这是一种臭名昭著的深度桌面游戏，可能需要数年时间才能学会和掌握。国际象棋中可能的走法数量如此之多，以至于创造了香农数字：估计10^120个可能的国际象棋棋盘结果。有抱负的国际象棋爱好者通常通过强化1：1的指导达到精通。
然而，今年早些时候，几位著名的国际象棋选手开始在Twitch上直播他们的比赛。观众们以现场解说和一步一步分析的形式，从一些世界顶尖棋手那里获得了免费的国际象棋课程。特级大师中村光与著名的英雄联盟和堡垒之夜职业选手对决，经常实时指导对手，吸引了50多万粉丝。当女子FIDE大师亚历山德拉·博特斯(Alexandra Botez)和她的妹妹共同主持一场Twitch国际象棋表演时，超过32万名追随者收看了她的比赛，并就国际象棋策略提出了问题。
在Botez和Nakamura等流媒体的帮助下，国际象棋吸引了新一代棋手。2020年，Twitch上的观看时长几乎每月翻了一番，仅今年一年就超过3600万小时。新的布道者包括顶级聊天流媒体人Felix“xQc”Lengyel和“权力的游戏”中的明星哈夫塔尔·J·比约恩松(“山”)。今天，国际象棋一直是Twitch上观看小时数排名前30的游戏，甚至超过了AAA级重量级游戏，如星际争霸2和最近上映的对马幽灵(Ghost Of Tsushima)。
D&amp；D是将直播作为指导手册的另一个理想案例研究。对于初学者来说，研发可能是一种令人望而生畏的经历。地图横跨多张纸，骰子有20个面，而不是通常的6个面，游戏过程持续数小时。玩家需要在几乎没有准备的情况下扮演他们分配的角色。D&amp；D规则手册也是出了名的迟钝--“玩家手册”厚达320页。简而言之，研发是很难自学的。
与国际象棋类似，直播也能够帮助解决这些入职挑战。在2015年D&amp；D；第五版发布后不久，玩家就开始直播他们的游戏过程。通过观看这些流，观众在娱乐的同时了解了游戏规则；没有比看到其他人玩真正的游戏(也会犯错)更好的老师了。观众还能够体验到一点来自即兴发挥的自发乐趣，这种感觉在单独阅读规则手册时很难把握。
通过这些渠道，D&amp；D成功地吸引了一批新的受众。自2015年以来，流传输的研发总时数每年翻一番。去年，仅在Twitch上，观众就观看了2000万小时的研发。
直播也扩大了D&A；D；的核心受众。今天40%的球员是女性(2012年为20%)，60%的球员年龄在25岁以上。还出现了一个新的研发影响力群体：Girls，Guts，Glory是一个广受欢迎的全女性研发团体，在大会上进行流媒体和角色扮演。关键角色(Critical Role)是一群专业配音演员，向超过50万粉丝播放D&A；D。他们2018年竞选活动的视频吸引了超过1.6亿的YouTube点击量。
长期以来，桌面游戏一直由它们的物理组成部分来定义，从卡坦定居者精美的手绘游戏板，到密密麻麻的文字魔术，从收集交易卡到中国跳棋的光滑弹珠，不一而足。然而，有时，这些物理部分可能会成为新来者进入的障碍，也是重放能力的限制因素。
例如，以“战锤”为例，这是一款桌面军事游戏，使用精雕细琢的微型雕像模拟军队之间的大规模军事战斗。每个球员都负责带着自己的军队去看比赛-平均军队规模从12个到100多个型号不等。灵魂战争，由创造者游戏工作室制作的顶级战锤首发部队套装，有52个微型模型和12个骰子(用于战斗)。这些微型模型通常是由它们的主人精心制作和手绘的。
由于所需时间和部件的投入，潜在的新玩家很难尝试或涉足战锤游戏。你要么需要拥有一支军队，要么需要借一支军队才能玩一场游戏。
为了缩小这一差距，视频游戏开发商对桌面游戏的部分内容进行了数字化重建和改进。创意集结的全面战争：战锤将游戏中的大规模战斗模拟为实时战略游戏，使玩家无需管理掷骰子或复杂的规则手册即可指挥军队。胖鲨的战锤：蚯蚓将玩家放入一个独立的英雄单位，以全新的第一人称视角与无穷无尽的怪物战斗。这两个专营权都是关键的成功，已经为战锤宇宙引入了超过1000万玩家。与此同时，自2016年推出第一款全面战争游戏以来，战锤的制造商游戏工作室的收入已经翻了一番多。
此外，随着视频游戏和桌面游戏的碰撞，用户生成的内容正悄然成为一股创造力的力量。像Roblox和“我的世界”这样的沙盒游戏已经允许玩家创造几乎任何他们能想象到的东西。同样，我们也看到沙盒创建工具与桌面游戏融合在一起。例如，游戏弹出式地下城使玩家可以创建他们想要的任何物品、能力或角色，然后用这些部分玩角色扮演游戏。这些规则是基于经典的桌面角色扮演游戏，但你几乎可以定制世界上的每一个地方。
在另一种方法中，Last Gameboard正在建造一款超大尺寸的平板电脑，允许玩家扫描定制的物理代币或卡片，并将其插入到数字棋盘游戏中。传统棋类游戏的内容受限于玩家所拥有的实体部分，而这些支持UGC的产品则开启了内容创作的大门，玩家的游戏行为仅受其想象力的限制。
用户生成内容的下一次演变可能是引入人工智能作为创作工具。为了一窥这可能是什么样子，人工智能地下城使用了尖端的GPT-3人工智能模型来生成D&amp；D地下城大师的脚本。随着GPT-3人工智能模型在自然语言处理方面的进步，大多数读者很难判断下面的段落是由人工智能还是人类作者撰写的。
随着AirPods和智能耳机变得无处不在，音频已经成为一个有价值的互动平台。音频的一个独特的好处是，它既可以作为你关注的前台活动，也可以作为你在做饭或遛狗时听的背景配乐。桌面游戏特别适合利用音频，因为它们在这两种模式下都能蓬勃发展。
作为一种前台活动，室内游戏，如琐事、字谜和黑手党，玩家可以猜单词、表演或辩论，都非常成功。由于以回合为基础的游戏和几乎不需要的实物材料，这些聚会游戏已经很好地转化为纯音频游戏。仅在Alexa平台上，“知识与琐事”部分就有超过1万款有声游戏，其中大部分是根据室内游戏改编的。经典之作，如“琐事”、“散漫”和“你更愿意”等，常年广受欢迎。歌曲问答是一款“给歌曲命名”的琐事游戏，它有一个基于云的音乐库，可以不断更新新歌。
作为一种后台活动，音频使用户能够在旅途中或在其他情况下无法直接玩游戏时体验他们最喜欢的游戏。例如，由于大型团队调度的挑战，D&amp；D通常每两周或每月上演一次。对于在小组会议间隙寻求研发的粉丝来说，音频产品提供了一种流线型的体验，既可以单独享受，也可以在通勤时享受。因为每一个动作都是在D&amp；D中大声讲述的，所以Alexa或Google可以有效地充当您的地下城主。多皮奥的“漩涡”是一款D&amp；D风格的科幻冒险游戏，专为音频设计。对于奇幻迷来说，魔术门是一款互动叙事游戏，在Alexa上有超过7500条评论。
播客也已经成为一种常规的互动工具，使游戏保持领先地位。粉丝们已经发现，研发活动可以以播客的形式录制和消费，而不会失去太多效力。因此，研发播客消费蓬勃发展。作为实验，地下城大师特拉维斯·麦克尔罗伊于2014年开始在“冒险区”中录制他家的研发过程。今天，特拉维斯的播客已经有超过1.5亿的下载量，两本衍生的“纽约时报”畅销漫画小说，以及一部正在制作中的动画电视连续剧。
特拉维斯并不孤单--目前有超过125个专注于研发的播客，更广泛地说，桌面游戏的播客数量是这个数字的两倍。总而言之，这些播客帮助用户保持对他们最喜欢的桌面游戏的投入，即使在不能直接玩的情况下也是如此。
展望未来，一些开发者创造了创新的混合体验，将最好的桌面游戏与播客风格的音频解说结合在一起。例如，僵尸，奔跑！是一款无视分类的混合音频健身游戏。
从表面上看，“僵尸”看起来像是一部D&amp；D风格的故事，你在其中扮演僵尸疫情中的幸存者的角色。不同的是，故事只有在听众运行的时候才会播放。这款应用程序可以检测你的位置，跟踪你的速度，并模拟你需要逃离僵尸的任务。有了深度RPG系统，这款游戏提供了一种锻炼，同时让玩家保持长期上钩。自发布以来，僵尸已经被超过500万跑步者使用，在6月份的App Store健康与健身类别中，它是下载量最大的应用程序。
桌面游戏的一个重要特点就是专注于小组游戏。大多数比赛都是以只有2到8人的小组进行的。因此，桌面游戏社区在历史上一直是支离破碎和高度本地化的。然而，在过去的几年里，社区平台和在线游戏的兴起逐渐克服了这些障碍，带来了网络效应的发挥。
以魔术：聚会为例：魔术发明于1993年，是最古老和最受欢迎的交易纸牌游戏之一，玩家收集并建造一副纸牌来对抗对手。许多魔术牌都是限量印刷的，这意味着不同的玩家通常会有非常不同的牌组和策略。这种变化无常鼓励不断与新对手较量。
然而，在魔术历史的大部分时间里，想要与其他爱好者见面的玩家仅限于在当地的游戏店或便士拱廊博览会(Penny Arade EXPO，简称PAX)等会议上会面。这款游戏的创造者，海岸奇才，已经与全球6000多家游戏商店合作，组织每周一次的魔术锦标赛，被粉丝亲切地称为“周五之夜魔术”。虽然这个数字看起来令人印象深刻，但它远远达不到总需求-今天有超过3500万人玩魔术。
D&amp；D也有类似的问题：有兴趣尝试这款游戏的新玩家经常被告知在Facebook上搜索群组或聚会，寻找附近的D&amp；D聚会。这种进入门槛可能让许多对玩这个游戏感兴趣的人望而却步，但他们被需要与数十个陌生人进行长达数小时的会议吓倒了。
对于魔术(Magic)和D&amp；D等超本地化桌面游戏而言，Discord和Reddit等在线社区平台的崛起改变了游戏规则。在过去的十年里，这些平台已经能够将支离破碎的群体缝合成一个互联的在线社区。官方DND Subreddit拥有200多万会员；MTG Subreddit拥有50多万会员。有4500多个不一致服务器专门用于D&amp；D或Magic，成千上万的玩家在那里互相聊天，谈论他们最喜欢的纸牌和战役。第一次玩游戏的人可以在舒适的家里结交朋友，讨论策略，收听现场比赛。
一旦玩家有兴趣尝试一款游戏，远程协作工具的爆炸式增长推动了在线游戏的发展。具有屏幕共享功能的视频聊天应用程序，如Zoom和Squad，使您可以在玩游戏时轻松地与朋友聊天。虚拟桌面平台，如Roll20和Tablettop Simulator，提供了玩游戏所需的所有工具，一直到虚拟骰子(滚动时都有声音效果)。像StartPlaying这样的在线市场甚至可以让玩家租用一个地下城大师来玩游戏。
对于更休闲的玩家来说，有各种新的数字选项可以轻松地提升到完整的体验。暴雪的炉石于2014年推出，是基于暴雪IP宇宙的魔术的精神继承者。凭借简化的系统和以华丽的3D动画呈现的纸牌，炉石迅速成长为当今最受欢迎的数字纸牌游戏之一，拥有超过1亿玩家。
像炉石这样的全数字游戏已经接触到了更新、更随意的纸牌游戏受众。由于在线网络效应和它们撮合以前遥远的玩家的能力，这些游戏的增长速度比它们的线下同行快得多。
此外，在线上线下网络效果的飞轮中，这些数字游戏还充当了原始桌面游戏的登机坡道。在2019年的大部分时间里，魔术聚会的店内参与率都以两位数的百分比增长。魔术最新的卡片扩展套装，4月份宣布的Ikoria：Neir of Behablers，已经在YouTube上获得了创纪录的2900万点击量。
传统的桌面游戏正在被提供创新的新游戏体验的现代数字工具戏剧性地改进和重新发明。迟钝的规则手册可以通过直播来克服。用户生成的数字内容可以提供无穷无尽的可重播性。音频产品创造了持续的参与度循环，尽管播放会话并不频繁。碎片化的用户群可以聚合成具有很强网络效应的在线社区。最终的结果是，桌面游戏比以往任何时候都能接触到更多的观众。
随着桌面游戏和视频游戏的碰撞，社交游戏的下一个重大创新越来越有可能是两者的融合。随着D&amp；D；、《战锤》和《魔术》等经典游戏都取得了前所未有的数字推动下的成功，问题不是是否，而是何时和如何。

note_en:
Dungeons & Dragons, the granddaddy of all role-playing games, has never been more popular than it is today. Released in 1976 the tabletop fantasy game was unique for introducing user-generated storytelling. One person dubbed the Dungeon Master prepares and narrates a story to a group of players who role-play characters in that fantasy. Like a “choose-your-own-adventure” book, the narrative unfolds based upon the collective choices of the group. At the time of its release,  D&D was a relatively niche game enjoyed mostly by ardent role-playing fans.
 Today, more than   40 million  people play  D&D around the world and sales have grown by   double-digit  percentages for the last five consecutive years. No longer a fringe hobby,  D&D features prominently in   mainstream  and   celebrity  culture (call it the “Stranger Things” bump).
 D&D’s growth is illustrative of a larger trend. Tabletop games—a quintessentially analog experience that encompasses board games, card games, and parlor games—are being dramatically improved by digital tools. While the first attempts at modernizing tabletop games sought to merely replicate games in the digital realm, the next generation of games goes a step further, integrating tools such as live-streaming, user generated content (UGC), audio products, and community platforms. This digital transformation is reinventing the way we learn, play, and connect with one another over tabletop games.
 As a result, modern tabletop games have the potential to be bigger than ever before. Board games are resurgent—the overall market reached   $12 billion  in 2018 and is growing at a 9 percent compound annual growth rate. Parlor games such as   Werewolf  and   Mafia , in which players act and deceive each other,  have grown into global hits. China’s   Werewolf Kill  app reached over   70 million  users within a year of launch. Even one of the oldest tabletop games, chess, is making a mighty comeback:   605 million  people around the world play today, and traffic to the official   chess.com  website roughly doubled  in the first half of the year to   94 million  monthly visitors.
 The next generation of tabletop games will diverge from tradition in four key ways: livestreaming, user-generated content, audio-first experiences, and online community platforms.
  Livestreaming—the broadcasting of real-time video on platforms like Twitch, YouTube, and Caffeine—has reached new heights of popularity, with over   7.5 billion  hours watched in Q2 alone this year. But though the medium has been widely adopted as a mode of entertainment, it’s also an extraordinarily effective means of teaching complex activities.
 Take chess, for example, a notoriously deep tabletop game that can take years to learn and master. The number of possible moves in chess is so large that the   Shannon number  was coined: an estimate of the 10^ 120  total possible chess board outcomes. Aspiring chess enthusiasts usually reached mastery through intensive 1:1 instruction.
 Yet earlier this year, several prominent chess players started livestreaming their games on Twitch. Viewers received free chess lessons in the form of live commentary and move-by-move analysis from some of the top chess players in the world. Grandmaster   Hikaru Nakamura  drew over half a million followers by playing against notable   League of Legends  and   Fortnite  pros, often mentoring opponents on stream in real-time. When women’s   FIDE  master   Alexandra Botez  co-hosted a Twitch chess show with her sister, more than 320,000 followers tuned in to watch her play and ask questions on chess strategy.
 With the help of streamers like Botez and Nakamura, chess has hooked a new generation of players. Hours watched on Twitch have nearly   doubled  every month in 2020, totaling over   36 million  hours this year alone. New evangelists include top   Just Chatting  streamer Felix “  xQc ” Lengyel and   Game of Thrones  star   Hafþór J Björnsson  (“The Mountain”). Today, chess consistently trends as a top 30 game on Twitch in terms of hours watched, topping even AAA heavyweights such as   Starcraft 2  and the recently released   Ghost of Tsushima .
 D&D is another ideal case study for livestreaming as an instruction manual. For first-timers, D&D can be a daunting experience. The map spans multiple sheets of paper, the dice have 20 sides instead of the usual 6, and the game sessions last multiple hours. Players are expected to role-play their assigned characters with little to no preparation. The  D&D rulebooks are also famously obtuse—the   Player’s Handbook  clocks in at a hefty 320 pages. In short,  D&D is very difficult to learn on your own.
 Similar to chess, livestreaming has been able to help address these onboarding challenges. Shortly after the release of  D&D’s fifth edition in 2015, players started livestreaming their play sessions. By watching these streams, viewers learned the rules of the game while being entertained; there’s no better teacher than seeing other humans play real games (and make mistakes). Viewers were also able to experience a bit of the spontaneous fun that comes from improvisational play, a feeling that’s difficult to grasp when reading rulebooks alone.
  Through these streams,  D&D has managed to capture a fresh audience. Total hours of  D&D streamed has   doubled  every year since 2015. Last year, viewers watched   20  million hours of  D&D on Twitch alone.
 Livestreaming has also broadened  D&D’s core audience.   Forty percent  of players today are female (up from   20 percent  in 2012) and   60 percent  of players are older than 25. A new class of  D&D influencers has also emerged:   Girls, Guts, Glory  is a popular all-female  D&D group that streams and cosplays at conventions.   Critical Role , a group of professional voice actors, streams  D&D to an audience of over half a million fans. Videos from their 2018 campaign drew over   160 million  YouTube views.
  Tabletop games have long been defined by their physical components, from the beautifully hand-drawn gameboard of   Settlers of Catan  to the densely written text of   Magic the Gathering  trading cards to the smooth marbles of Chinese Checkers. Yet, at times, these physical parts can be a barrier to entry for newcomers, as well as a limiting factor on replayability.
 For example, take    Warhammer , a tabletop wargame that simulates large-scale military battles between armies using intricately carved miniature figurines. Each player is responsible for bringing their own army to a match—the average army size ranges from a dozen to over one hundred models.  Soul Wars, the top  Warhammer starter army set produced by   Creator Games Workshop,  counts 52 miniatures and 12 dice (used for combat). These miniatures are usually meticulously built and hand-painted by their owners.
  Due to the investment in time and parts required, it is very difficult for a potential new player to try out or dabble in   Warhammer . You need to either own or borrow an army to play even a single game.
 To close this gap, video game developers have recreated and improved upon parts of the tabletop game digitally. Creative Assembly’s    Total War: Warhammer  simulates the franchise’s large-scale battles as a real-time strategy game, enabling players to command an army without managing dice rolls or complex rulebooks. Fatshark’s    Warhammer: Vermintide  places players into an individual hero unit that battles endless hordes of monsters in a fresh, first-person perspective. Both franchises are critical successes that have introduced over   10 million  players to the   Warhammer  universe. In the meantime,  Warhammer‘s maker, Games Workshop, has seen revenue more than   double  since the first   Total War  game launched in 2016.
 Furthermore, as video games and tabletop games collide, user-generated content is quietly emerging as a force of creativity. Sandbox games such as   Roblox  and   Minecraft  already allow players to create just about anything they can dream up. In a similar vein, we’re seeing sandbox creation tools merge with tabletop games. For example, the game    Popup Dungeon  enables players to create any item, ability, or character they want, then play a role-playing game with those parts. The rules are based on classic tabletop role-playing games, but you can customize almost every part of the world.
 In another approach,  The Last Gameboard is building a supersized tablet that allows players to scan and insert custom physical tokens or cards into a digital board game. Whereas content in a traditional board game is limited by the physical parts that a player owns, these UGC-enabled products open up content creation so that a player’s gameplay is limited only by their imagination.
  The next evolution of user-generated content will likely be the introduction of AI as a creation tool. For a glimpse at what this could look like,  AI Dungeon uses a cutting-edge GPT-3 AI model to generate scripts for  D&D Dungeon Masters. With the GPT-3 AI model’s advances in natural language processing, most readers would be hard pressed to tell if the passage below was written by an AI or a human author.
   As Airpods and smart headphones have become ubiquitous, audio has emerged as a   valuable engagement platform . One of the unique benefits of audio is that it can serve as both a foreground activity that you focus on, as well as a background soundtrack that you listen to as you’re cooking or walking the dog. Tabletop games are uniquely suited to capitalize on audio, due to their ability to thrive in both modalities.
 As a foreground activity, parlor games such as  Trivia,    Charades  , and    Mafia , in which players guess words, act, or debate, have been very successful. With turn-based play and few physical materials needed, these party games have translated well to audio-only play. On the Alexa platform alone, there are over   10,000  audio games in the “Knowledge and Trivia” section, most of which are adaptations of parlor games. Classics such as    Trivia ,    Scattergories , and    Would You Rather  are perennially popular.    Song Quiz  is a “name-that-song” trivia game with a cloud-based music library that is constantly updated with new songs.
 As a background activity, audio enables users to experience their favorite games on-the-go or when they would otherwise be unable to directly play.  D&D, for example, is usually played biweekly or monthly due to the challenges of large group scheduling. For fans seeking  D&D in between group sessions, audio products provide a streamlined experience that can be enjoyed solo or on a commute. Since every action is narrated aloud in  D&D, Alexa or Google can serve effectively as your Dungeon Master. Doppio’s    The Vortex  is a D&D-style sci-fi choose-your-own-adventure game designed for audio. For fantasy fans,    The Magic Door  is an interactive narrative game with over 7,500 reviews on Alexa.
 Podcasts have also emerged as a regular engagement tool that enables games to stay top-of-mind. Fans have discovered that  D&D campaigns can be recorded and consumed in podcast form without losing much of their potency. As a result,  D&D podcast consumption has blossomed. Dungeon Master Travis McElroy started recording his family’s  D&D sessions in “  The Adventure Zone ” in 2014 as an experiment. Today, Travis’s podcast has over   150 million  downloads, two spin-off  New York Times best-selling   graphic novels , and an animated TV series in the works.
 Travis isn’t alone—there are   more than 125  D&D-focused podcasts in existence, and twice that number for tabletop games more broadly. Collectively, these podcasts help users stay engaged with their favorite tabletop games, even when unable to play directly.
 Looking to the future, some developers have created innovative hybrid experiences that combine the best of tabletop games with podcast-style audio narration.    Zombies, Run! , for example, is a hybrid audio-fitness game that defies categorization.
  On the surface,   Zombies  looks like a  D&D-style narrative in which you play the role of a survivor in a zombie epidemic. The twist is that the story only plays out while the listener is running. The app detects your location, tracks your speed, and simulates   missions  where you need to run from zombies. With deep RPG systems, the game provides a workout while keeping players hooked for the long run. Since launch,   Zombies  has been used by over   5 million  runners and was the #1 most downloaded app in the App Store’s Health & Fitness category in June.
  One of the defining traits of tabletop games has been their focus on small group play. Most games are played in groups of only two to eight people. As a result, the tabletop games community has historically been fragmented and hyper-local. Yet over the last few years, the rise of community platforms and online play has gradually overcome these barriers and brought   network effects  to play.
 Take   Magic: The Gathering  as an example: Invented in 1993,   Magic  is one of the oldest and most popular trading card games, where players collect and build decks of cards to play against opponents. Many   Magic  cards are printed in limited quantities, meaning that different players will often have wildly different decks and strategies. This variability encourages constant play against new opponents.
  Yet for most of   Magic’ s  history, players wanting to meet fellow enthusiasts were limited to meet-ups at their local game store or conventions like the   Penny Arcade Expo  (PAX). The game’s creator, Wizards of the Coast, has partnered with over   6,000  game stores worldwide to organize weekly   Magic  tournaments, affectionately dubbed “Friday Night Magic” by fans. While this number may seem impressive, it falls far short of total demand—over   35 million  people play   Magic  today.
 D&D has a similar problem: new players interested in trying out the game were often told to search Facebook Groups or MeetUps for neighborhood  D&D gatherings. This barrier to entry likely dissuaded many who were interested in playing the game, but were intimidated by the need to show up for an hours-long meeting with dozens of strangers.
 For hyper-local tabletop games like   Magic  and  D&D, the rise of online community platforms like Discord and Reddit has been a game-changer. Over the past decade, these platforms have been able to stitch together fragmented groups into a connected online community. The official   DnD  subreddit counts over 2 million members; the   MTG  subreddit counts over 500,000. There are more than   4,500  Discord servers dedicated to  D&D or   Magic , where tens of thousands of players chat with each other about their favorite cards and campaigns. First-time players can make friends, discuss strategy, and listen in on live games from the comfort of their homes.
 Once a player is interested in trying out a game, the explosion of tools for remote collaboration has propelled online play. Video chat apps with screen-sharing functionality such as   Zoom  and   Squad  make it easy to chat with friends while playing. Virtual tabletop platforms such as   Roll20  and   Tabletop Simulator  provide all the tools needed to play, down to virtual dice (with sound effects as they roll). And online marketplaces such as   StartPlaying  even enable players to rent a Dungeon Master for a game.
 For more casual players, there are a variety of new digital options to ease the ramp-up to the complete experience. Blizzard’s   Hearthstone  launched in 2014 as a spiritual successor to   Magic  based upon the Blizzard IP universe. With simplified systems and cards rendered in gorgeous 3D animations,   Hearthstone  quickly grew to become one of the most popular digital card games today, with over   100 million  players.
  Fully digital games like   Hearthstone  have reached a newer, more casual audience for trading card games. These games have grown much faster than their offline counterparts due to online network effects and their ability to matchmake previously far-flung players.
 Furthermore, in a flywheel of online-offline network effects, these digital games also serve as an onboarding ramp for the original tabletop game. In-store participation for   Magic  gatherings grew by   double-digit  percentages for most of 2019.   Magic’s  latest card expansion set,   Ikoria: Lair of Behemoths , announced in April, has already racked up a record breaking   29 million  YouTube views.
  Traditional tabletop games are being dramatically improved—and reinvented—by modern digital tools that provide innovative new playing experiences. Obtuse rulebooks can be overcome through livestreaming. User-generated digital content can provide endless replayability. Audio products create sustained engagement loops, despite infrequent play sessions. And a fragmented user base can be aggregated into online communities with strong network effects. The net result is that tabletop games are reaching larger audiences than ever before.
 As tabletop games and video games collide, it becomes increasingly likely that the next big innovation in social play will be a merger of the two. With classic games like   D&D ,   Warhammer , and   Magic  all reaching unprecedented levels of digitally-propelled success, the question is not if,  but when and how.

title: 传统的桌面游戏正在被数字工具(如直播、UGC和音频)重新改造，以提供新的体验并接触到更多的观众
title_en: Traditional tabletop games are being reinvented by digital tools such as live-streaming, UGC, and audio to provide new experiences and reach larger audiences

note:
纳西姆·塔勒布最出名的是他的书，特别是“黑天鹅”，但他也以令人难忘的表情而闻名。他们都很受人尊敬，他把他们都放进了一本书里。它们很短，通常是反向的，通常很有娱乐性。以下是几个例子：
你可以通过问一个人觉得他感兴趣的人来判断他有多无趣。
那些不认为就业是系统性奴隶制的人要么是盲人，要么是有工作的人。
罗马和奥斯曼帝国时期的奴隶与今天的雇员的不同之处在于，奴隶不需要奉承他们的老板。
除了是逆向投资者外，他们还常常是刻薄的、精力充沛的或充满敌意的。例如，在第一本书中，他称那些他在批评的人是傻瓜，第二本书他试图进入一个多么无趣的人，第三本书和第四本书中，他将工资就业与奴隶制进行了相当粗鲁的比较。第五种是经典的[X]是[A]，[Y]是[！A]，这是另一个流行的Taleb模式。
请注意，这些例子不是我精心挑选的。所有这些引语都来自他的格言书的维基百科页面。这些涵盖了他流行表达的一些流行主题。
我过去写过几篇关于GPT-3的文章，GPT-3是OpenAI的预测性自然语言模型。因此，我用一些Taleb引语来提示模型，看看我们是否可以通过尝试生成一些Taleb引语来从模型或Taleb中得出任何见解。
正如在上一篇文章中提到的，GPT-3的工作方式是提供提示并要求模型继续文本。
在玩弄模型的过程中，我发现提示符在不同的方面既重要又不重要。重要是，如果您希望您的模型生成html，您必须提供一些html，或者至少指示创建一个带有html的问题/答案类型提示符。但你并不需要比这更多的东西了。也就是说，您不需要提供详尽的提示符。它需要的东西非常少。
如上所述，我不认为它特别重要，但以下是我使用的提示：
塔勒布：“错过火车只有在你追赶它的时候才是痛苦的！同样，如果这就是你想要的，与别人期望你成功的想法不匹配才是痛苦的。塔勒布：“魅力是侮辱别人而不冒犯他们的能力；书呆子正好相反。”塔勒布说：“最有害的三种瘾是海洛因、碳水化合物和月薪。”塔勒布：“一半的人用嘴唇撒谎，另一半的人用眼泪撒谎。”塔勒布说：
我从几个主题中选择了一句话：逆向思维，对挣工资的批评，对碳水化合物的仇恨，对人的侮辱。
如果选项具有正伽马，则增量将为负；反之亦然。
Taleb之前是一名期权交易员，但所有提示都没有提到金融、期权或数学，所以我想这个模型是Taleb：在提示中透露的。这是我在GPT-3中注意到的，人和地点可以显著影响输出，而不仅仅是主题。我去掉了Taleb的提示符，得到的是不那么刻薄的精力充沛的报价，没有关于金融或期权交易的。
“音乐是生命，它表达的情感太深，无法用语言表达。”“名声就像一条河，越小越少，越大越难渡过。”“下雨的时候，最好的办法就是让雨滴打在你的头上，给你好好洗一洗，洗干净你的大脑。”
您可能会对此印象深刻，该模型对单词Taleb有很好的向量表示，可以相当容易地匹配他的风格和主题。但从另一个角度来看，它已经记住了Taleb风格的引语，而不是从我提供的实际引语中推断出风格。
1.。“为没有领导的生活而悲伤是无用的，为被误导的生活而悲伤更好。”“好的风险经理人对世界没有乐观/悲观的看法--他们有现实的看法。这是一个非常艰难的问题。“。“人类是一种吵闹的动物。当他说话的时候，他会说些什么。当他沉默的时候，他会说得更多。“你不需要是一个知识分子来决定世界的命运，但是你确实需要一个知识分子来理解这个世界。”“金融不是专业知识。任何自称是专家的人都意味着他是个白痴。6.一个男人穿了20年同一件外套，要么是忠诚的男人，要么是懒惰的男人；一个留了20年同一种发型的女人，要么是圣人，要么是妓女。(6)一个男人穿了20年同一件外套，要么是忠诚的男人，要么是懒惰的男人；如果女人坚持了20年，不是圣人就是妓女。
1到3是经典的塔勒布风格，不是这个，而是那个。没有什么特别的或值得注意的。
4很有趣，因为它似乎与塔勒布所说的相去甚远。即使是塔勒布的不经意的追随者也知道塔勒布对现代“知识分子”、叙事偏见和反对地方主义的中央规划非常挑剔。塔勒布甚至创造了“聪明但愚蠢”或“IYI”这个短语。所以，虽然这句话是塔勒布风格的，但它在很大程度上是一句反塔勒布的话。
5是几个Taleb主题、金融、专业知识和称人为白痴的混合体。
“一个男人穿同一件外套二十年，不是忠诚的男人，就是懒惰的男人；一个留了二十年同一种发型的女人，不是圣人就是妓女。”
这很像塔勒布的风格。它有经典的[X]是[A]，[Y]是[！A]，是侮辱性的，粗鲁的，可以说是性别歧视。但这意味着什么呢，还是仅仅是一个复杂的塔勒布主题的疯狂解放运动？
男人穿外套，女人留发型？它们都是自己的表现吗？它们都是不常更换的物品吗？他们都会在时尚或品味上受到社会变化的影响吗？
20年后会发生什么呢？据推测，20年是从成年(~18岁)开始的，所以20年应该是从18岁到38年。在这段时间里，许多人完成了学业，找到了工作，找到了伴侣，生了孩子。这段时间的结束标志着你的生育年龄的结束。
一个男人长时间穿同一件外套，要么忠诚，要么懒惰，这第一句话似乎有些道理。我不认为“忠诚”可以用在一件衣服上，但它可能意味着男人不受潮流的影响，所以这可以被解读为他的信念是绝对的和忠诚的，当然也可以是懒惰的。
第二个关于女人和发型的陈述就不那么清楚了。我们可以假设，这种发型只是外套的替身，很少改变，是时代的象征。而穿外套对女性来说就不太合适了，因为女性通常购物更多，一位穿了20年大衣的女性在这段时间里穿大衣的可能性要比男性小得多。圣徒对妓女可能只是忠诚的替身。
但是运用上面的推理，为什么男人的一致性会归因于美德的原因或平庸的原因，而女人的一致性则归因于美德或罪恶呢？也许女性在她们的选择上更加慎重，或者至少在时尚的选择上是如此。
归根结底，我认为它更像是一个老练的疯子。我用单词“Taleb”提示这个模型，这让它大放异彩，而且大多数生成的引语都符合相同的、略显肤浅的模式。即使是我觉得发人深省的最后一句话，本身也不是一句有意义的格言。它带有侮辱性，节奏很好，但我没能理解它的意思。
那么，GPT-3只是一个复杂的模式匹配模型吗？我想不止于此，但也没什么大不了的。
note_en:
Nassim Taleb is best known for his books, particularly  Black Swan, but he’s also known for his memorable expressions. They are so well regarded, he put them all in a  book . They’re short, often contrarian and usually entertaining. Here’s a few:
 You can tell how uninteresting a person is by asking him whom he finds interesting.
 Those who do not think that employment is systemic slavery are either blind or employed.
 The difference between slaves in Roman and Ottoman days and today’s employees is that slaves did not need to flatter their boss.
 Apart from being contrarian, they are often mean spirited or adversarial. For instance, in the first one he calls those he’s criticizing fools, the second he’s trying to access how  uninteresting a person and the third and fourth ones he makes a rather rude comparison of  wage employment to slavery. The fifth one is classic [X] is [A], [Y] is [!A], another popular Taleb pattern.
 And please note that I didn’t cherry pick these examples. All these quotes are from the  wikipedia page of his  book of aphorisms. And these cover some popular motifs of his popular expressions.
 I’ve written a  few  times in the past about GPT-3, the predictive natural language model by OpenAI. So I prompted the model with some Taleb quotes to see if we can draw any insights from the model or Taleb by trying to generate some Taleb quotes.
   As mentioned in a  prior post, GPT-3 works by providing a prompt and asking the model to continue the text.
 From playing around with the model, I found that the prompt is both important and unimportant in different ways. It’s important in the sense that if you want your model to produce html, you have to provide some html or at least indicate create a question/answer type prompt with html indicated. But you don’t really need much more than that. Meaning, you don’t need to provide an extensive elaborate prompt. It needs remarkably little.
 As mentioned above, I don’t think its super important, but here is the prompt I used:
 taleb: “Missing a train is only painful if you run after it! Likewise, not matching the idea of success others expect from you is only painful if that’s what you are seeking.” taleb: “Charm is the ability to insult people without offending them; nerdiness the reverse” taleb: “The three most harmful addictions are heroin, carbohydrates, and a monthly salary.” taleb: “Half of the people lie with their lips; the other half with their tears” taleb:
 I selected a quote from a few themes: contrarian thinking, criticism of earning a salary, hatred of carbs, insulting people.
  “If an option has a positive gamma, the delta will be negative; the reverse is not true”
 Taleb previously worked as an options trader, but none of the prompts mentioned finance, options or math, so I imagine the model was tipped off by  taleb: in the prompt. That’s something I noticed in GPT-3 that people and places can significantly influence the output, not just the themes. I removed the  taleb prompt and got much less mean spirited quotes and none about finance or option trading.
  “Music is life that expresses emotions too deep for words.” “Fame is like a river: the smaller it is, the less it possesses, the bigger it is, the more difficult it is to cross” “The best thing one can do when it’s raining is to let the raindrops hit your head, give you a good wash and clean your brain”
 You could be impressed by this that the model has such a good vector representation of the word  taleb that it can match his style and themes fairly easily. But another way to look at it is that it has memorized Taleb style quotes, and doesn’t infer the style from the actual quotes I provided.
  1. “Useless to grieve a life not led; better to grieve over a life led wrongly.” 2. “Good risk managers do not have an optimist/pessimist view of the world – they have a realistic view. And it is a very tough one.” 3. “Man is a noisy animal. When he speaks, he says something. When he’s silent, he says even more.” 4. “You don’t need to be an intellectual to shape the world’s destiny, but you do need intellectualism to understand the world.” 5. “Finance is not an expertise. Anyone who claims to be an expert means he is an idiot. Anything that anyone claims to know means he knows nothing.” 6. "A man who wears the same coat for 20 years is either a loyal man or a lazy one; a woman who stays with the same hairstyle for 20 years is either a saint or a whore"
 1 through 3 is classic Taleb style,  not this, but that. Nothing very remarkable or note worthy.
 4 is interesting because it seems way off from something Taleb would say. Even the casual follower of Taleb knows Taleb is very critical of modern day “intellectuals”, narrative bias and central planning opposed to localism. Taleb even coined the phrase “intellectual yet idiot” or IYI. So although the quote is in Taleb style, it’s very much an anti-Taleb quote.
 5 is a mashup of a few Taleb themes, finance, expertise and calling people idiots.
  “A man who wears the same coat for 20 years is either a loyal man or a lazy one; a woman who stays with the same hairstyle for 20 years is either a saint or a whore”
 It’s much in the style of Taleb. It’s got the classic [X] is [A], [Y] is [!A], is insulting, crude and arguably sexist. But does it mean anything or is it just a sophisticated Taleb themed mad-libs?
   Men wears a coat while woman wears a hairstyle? They’re both expressions of themselves? They’re both articles that change somewhat infrequently? They’re both subject to societal shifts in fashion or taste?
 What happens over 20 years? Presumably the 20 years starts at adulthood (~18) so twenty years would be from 18 — 38. During that time, many people finish school, find employment, find a mate and have children. The end of that period marks the end of your child-bearing age.
 The first statement of a man wearing the same coat for a long time being either loyal or lazy is somewhat plausible. I don’t think “loyalty” could be applied to an article of clothing, but it could mean that the man is not swayed by trends, so that could be interpreted as being absolute in his convictions and loyal, or of course lazy.
 The second statement about a woman and a hairstyle is less clear. We can assume that the hairstyle is just a stand-in for coat, something that changes infrequently and is a symbol of the times. And using coat would be less appropriate for a woman since women stereotypically shop more and a woman having the same coat for 20 years would be much less likely than a man having a coat for that period of time. And saint vs whore could just be a stand-in for loyalty.
 But using the reasoning above, why would consistency in men be attributed to a virtuous reason or a banal reason, while that same consistency in women be attributed to a virtue or a sin? Perhaps women are a lot more deliberate in their choices, or at least the choices of fashion.
 In the end I think its more of a sophisticated mad-libs. The model gleams a lot from the fact that I prompt it with the word “Taleb”, and most of the generated quotes fit the same somewhat shallow pattern. Even the last quote, which I found thought provoking, doesn’t stand on its own as a meaningful aphorism. Its insulting and has a good rhythm, but I wasn’t able to decipher its meaning.
 So is GPT-3 just a sophisticated pattern matching model? I think it’s more than that, but not much more.
title: 美丽，黑暗，扭曲，GPT-3产生了纳西姆·塔勒布的格言
title_en: The Beautiful, Dark, Twisted, GPT-3 Generated Nassim Taleb Aphorisms

美国有线电视新闻网(CNN)-研究人员成功地进行了Fly-V的处女航，这是一种未来主义的节油飞机，有朝一日可以用机翼搭载乘客。
Fliding-V的独特设计将客舱、货舱和燃料箱放置在机翼上，专家们希望这架飞机的空气动力学形状将比现在的飞机减少20%的燃料消耗。“飞行-V&V”的独特设计将客舱、货舱和燃料箱放在机翼上，专家们希望这种飞机的空气动力学形状将比现在的飞机减少20%的燃料消耗。
专家们测试了由荷兰代尔夫特理工大学(Delft University Of Technology)和合作伙伴荷兰航空公司(KLM)的研究人员开发的22.5公斤、3米比例的未来飞机模型，以带领这架备受期待的飞机沿着下一步的发展步伐前进。
一组研究人员和工程师在德国一个戒备森严的空军基地测试了这架飞机，他们在那里与空客团队合作，测试起飞、机动和进场，以及降落。
领导该项目的代尔夫特科技大学(Delft‘s University of Technology)航空工程学院助理教授罗洛夫·沃斯(Roelof Vos)在一份声明中解释说，我们担心的一个问题是，飞机在起飞时可能会遇到一些困难，因为之前的计算表明，旋转可能是一个问题。
研究小组优化了比例飞行模型，以防止这个问题，但布丁的证据在于吃。他说，你需要坐飞机才能确定。
他们指出，通过遥控飞机，研究人员设法以每小时80公里的速度起飞，而飞机的飞行速度、角度和推力都按计划进行。
专家们努力优化飞机：为了改进遥测，团队被迫改变了飞机的重心，调整了天线。
在飞机可以搭载乘客升空之前，还有很多工作要做：研究人员表示，试飞表明，这架飞机目前的设计允许过多的荷兰式侧滚，这会导致着陆不稳。
专家们计划将从试飞中收集的数据用于飞机的空气动力学模型，允许他们在飞行模拟器中对其进行编程，用于未来的测试，并改善飞行。该团队将对该模型进行更多测试，并希望为Fly-V提供可持续的推进，因为该设计适合携带液氢而不是煤油。
note_en:
(CNN) —  Researchers have conducted a successful maiden flight of the  Flying-V, a futuristic and fuel efficient airplane that could one day carry passengers in its wings.
 The Flying-V's unique design places the  passenger cabin, the cargo hold and the fuel tanks in the wings, and experts hope that the plane's aerodynamic shape will cut fuel consumption by 20% compared to today's aircrafts.
  Experts tested a 22.5 kg and 3-meter scale model of the futuristic airplane, developed by researchers at Delft University of Technology in the Netherlands and partner Dutch airline KLM, to take the highly anticipated aircraft along its next developmental steps.
  A team of researchers and engineers tested the aircraft at a guarded airbase in Germany, where they worked with an Airbus team to test takeoffs, maneuvers and approaches, and landing.
  "One of our worries was that the aircraft might have some difficulty lifting-off, since previous calculations had shown that 'rotation' could be an issue," Roelof Vos, assistant professor at the aerospace engineering faculty of Delft's University of Technology, who led the project,  explained in a statement.
 "The team optimized the scaled flight model to prevent the issue but the proof of the pudding is in the eating. You need to fly to know for sure," he said.
  Remotely controlling the aircraft, researchers managed take off at a speed of 80 kmh, while the aircraft's flight speeds, angles and thrust were as planned, they noted.
 Experts worked hard to optimize the plane: in order to improve telemetry, the team was forced to change the aircraft's center of gravity and adjust its antenna.
  There is still work to be done to refine the aircraft before it could take to the skies with passengers aboard: researchers said that the test flight showed that the aircraft's current design allows for too much "Dutch roll," which causes a rough landing.
 Experts plan to use the data collected from the test flight for an aerodynamic model of the aircraft, allowing them to program it in a flight simulator for future tests, and to improve flights. The team will conduct more tests on the model, and hope to provide the Flying-V with sustainable propulsion, given that the design lends itself to carrying liquid hydrogen instead of kerosene.
title: “Fly-V”飞机首飞成功

note: 草稿A Berglas博士，2008年2月/10月，AXnthony@Berglas.org(欢迎反馈，删除X。)。随意复制，但提供署名。这篇文章大胆地挑战了长期以来的误解，即昂贵的软件项目的灾难性失败对社会是有害的。对澳大利亚税务局等官僚机构的历史分析表明，自20世纪50年代以来，大规模软件自动化并没有提高他们的实际效率。正如帕金森预测的那样，个人工作人员效率的任何提高都只是被增加的官僚复杂性所消耗；由于软件的主要净效应是促进官僚主义的复杂性，因此，如果社会要有效运作，软件项目失败是至关重要的。这样，无数项目经理就可以卸下负罪感的沉重负担，他们下意识地将自己的职业生涯投入到确保项目很少成功(如果有的话)的肩上。许多人哀叹，软件项目很少达到他们的期望，而且许多项目被完全放弃，代价高昂。当工程师们开始构建一个项目时，许多人都在抱怨这样一个事实：软件项目很少达到他们的期望，而且许多项目被完全放弃了，花费巨大。当工程师们开始构建一个项目时，他们很少(如果有的话)成功。许多人哀叹，软件项目很少达到他们的期望，而且许多项目被完全放弃，花费巨大。当工程师们开始构建一个。桥梁建设几乎总是成功的。建造的桥梁几乎总是功能正确，通常建造得相当接近预算。但当一大群程序员开始开发软件时，非常不确定是否会交付任何有用的东西。有些人将此归因于组织文化。*如果为了削减成本，工程师故意低于指定的承重梁，他们将被送进监狱。但当程序员削减拐角时，他们会得到提拔。其他人则认为，问题领域是根本不同的。《帕金森》(1955)是一部分析官僚机构成长的开创性著作。它证明了工作倍增定律，并提供了实证例子，包括英国海军部相对于船只数量的减少的增长，以及帝国衰落期间殖民地办事处的增长。该论文开发了依赖于众多参数准确预测任何官僚机构增长的科学公式，这些公式中没有一个与实际要执行的工作量有关。但帕金森在1955年写道，他不可能预见到计算机化自动化将在接下来的几十年里产生的巨大影响。本文用伯格拉斯的评分法更新了帕金森定律，即再多的自动化也不会对官僚机构的规模或效率产生任何重大影响。为了看看这一推论在实践中的效果，我们将考虑其基本职能自1955年以来一直没有改变的官僚机构，即。在计算机自动化之前。一个很好的例子是澳大利亚税务局。首先要自动化的是纳税申报单的基本处理。这包括计算到期税款，将它们与雇主文件相关联，以及打印和核对退款支票。这是在大型而昂贵的主机上执行的，这些主机的计算能力比现在基本的移动电话中的计算能力要小。但是，即使是一个古老的主机也可以手工完成数千名办事员的对账工作。这项工作是在计算机自动化之前进行的。澳大利亚税务局就是一个很好的例子。第一个自动化的方面是基本的纳税申报单的基本处理。这包括计算应缴税款，将其与雇主文件相关联，以及打印和核对退款支票。自动化现在已经扩展到税务局的各个方面。纳税申报单通过网络以电子方式输入，由许多复杂的计算机系统分析和处理，退款或付款通过直接的银行存款处理。大多数申报单从来不需要人工触摸。内部管理系统也高度自动化，从审计的分配和跟踪到处理内部工资和福利系统。基本的办公流程也得到了极大的改进。1955年，写备忘录是一项艰巨的任务。他们不得不写备忘录。而且任何错误都需要重新打印完整的文件。此外，除非使用耗时的盖斯特纳机器，否则只能复印三到四份，所有的复印件都必须手工分发。20世纪80年代文字处理机和复印机的引入意味着备忘录可以更容易地撰写和复印。20世纪90年代的电子邮件系统当时提供了一种机制，可以高效和即时地将备忘录分发给数十个(如果不是数百个)收件人。其影响是，在1955年，电子邮件系统提供了一种机制，可以将备忘录高效和即时地分发给数十个(如果不是数百个)的收件人。因此，在20世纪80年代，文字处理机和复印机的引入意味着备忘录可以更容易地撰写和复印。因此，考虑到自动化的巨大增长，问题就出现了，现代官僚机构是否可以在没有这种自动化的情况下运作？对于我们的例子来说，
note_en: DRAFT   Dr A Berglas, February/October 2008 AXnthony@Berglas.org  (Feedback welcome, remove X.) Copy at will but provide attribution.  This paper boldly challenges the long established misconception thatthe catastrophic failure of expensive software projects is detrimentalto society.  Historical analysis ofbureaucracies such as the Australian Tax Office shows that massivesoftware automation has not increased their real efficiency since the 1950s. Any increase in the efficiency of individualworkers has simply been consumed by increased bureaucraticcomplexity, as predicted by Parkinson's law.   As the primarynet effect of software is to facilitate bureaucratic complexity it istherefor essential that software projects fail if society is tofunctioneffectively.  In this way the heavy burden of guilt can be liftedfrom the shoulders of the numerous project managers that havesubconsciously devoted their careers to ensuring that projects rarely,if ever, succeed.   Many have bemoaned the fact that software projects rarely live up to theirexpectations, and many are completely abandoned at great expense. When engineers setout to build a bridge a bridge gets built.  Our water ways are notcluttered with abandoned structures that cannot be made to work; bridgebuilding is almost always successful.  And the constructed bridgesalmost always function correctly and are usually built fairly close tobudget.  Butwhen a large team of programers sets out to develop software it is veryuncertain whether anything of use will be delivered at all.    Some put this down to organizational culture.  If an engineer wereto deliberately under specify a load bearing beam in order to cutcosts, they would be sent to jail.  But when a programmer cuts acorner they get promoted.  Others suggest that the problemdomains are fundamentally different.  But all commentatorsassume that failure is detrimental to society.  This paper sets out tocorrect that misunderstanding.   Parkinson (1955)is a seminal work that analysis the growth of bureaucracy.  Itproves the Law of the Multiplication of Work, and provides empiricalexamples that include the growth of the British admiralty compared tothe decline in the number of ships, and the growth of the colonialoffices during the decline of the empire.  The paper developsscientific formulas that accurately predicts the growth of anybureaucracy depending on numerous parameters, none of which relate totheamount of work to actually be performed.  But writing in 1955, Parkinson could not have foreseen the massiveimpact that computerized automation would have in the followingdecades.  This paper updates Parkinson's law with Berglas'scorollary, namely that no amount of automation will have anysignificant effect on the size or efficiency of a bureaucracy.   To see the effect of the corollary in practice, we will considerbureaucracies whose essential function has remained unchanged since1955,ie. before computer automation.  A good example is the AustralianTax Office.    The first aspect to be automated was the basic processing of tax returns.  Thisincluded calculating tax due, correlating them with employer documents and the printing andreconciliation of refund cheques.  This was performed on largeand expensive mainframes that had less computing power than is nowfound in basic mobile telephones.  But even one ancient mainframecould perform the work of a thousand clerks reconciling accounts byhand.   Automation has now spread to all aspects of the tax office.  Taxreturns are entered electronically over the web, analyzed and processedby a number of complex computer systems, and refunds or payments areprocessed via direct bank deposits.  Most returns are nevertouched by a human hand.  The internal management systems are alsohighly automated, from the allocation and tracking of audits toprocessing their internal payroll and benefits systems.    Basic office processes have also been greatly improved.  Back in1955 writing a memo was a substantial undertaking.  They had to bewritten out by hand or dictated to a stenographer.  Then a typistfrom the pool had to be allocated, and any errors required the documentto be retyped in full.  Moreover only three or four copies couldbe made unless time consuming Gestetner machines were used, and allcopies had to be distributed by hand.     The introduction of the word processor and photocopier in the 1980smeant that memos could be written and copied far more easily. E-Mail systems in the 1990s then provided a mechanism for the efficientand instantaneous distribution of memos to dozens if not hundreds ofrecipients.  The effect of this is that in 1955 the average clerkwould only receive a handful of memos each week, and rarely writeone.  Whereas today even the most junior clerk can receive manydozens of emails every single day.  So given this huge increase in automation, the question arises as towhether a modern bureaucracy could function without thisautomation?  So for our example, suppose we took each and everycomputer out of the tax office, from personal computers to largemainframes, so that all the processing had to be performed completelymanually.  Could the tax office still function effectively withinthe same budget?  The answer is, obviously, yes they could.  We know thisbecause in2007 the tax office's internal budget was AU$11.4 billion, or 1.23% ofGDP.  In 1955 it performed essentially the same task withoutautomation for A66.7 million which was 1.33% of the 1955GDP.   The difference is not statistically significant. (Normalizing by GDP (essentially the sum of everyone's earnings)accounts for the growing population and inflation.)  To many this is a surprising result.  How could the staggeringamount of automation instigated over the previous fifty years notproduce any meaningful effect on productivity?  This will beexamined in the next section but it is an empirical and undeniable factthat bureaucracies have grown, not shrunk.  And for those that would be quick to blame government sloth, similarresults can also be shown for private enterprises.  For examplethe banking industry today performs essentially the same function as itdid in 1955, when bank accounts were all reconciled by hand.  Yetthe banking industry has grown 189% as a proportion of GDP.      The cartoon above was published in the early 1970s (in CreativeComputing).  It reflected the widespread fear and dismay at thetime amongst bureaucrats who understood the enormous power of even asingle mainframe computer.   However, with an understandingof Berglas's Corollary they now know that they never had anything tofear.  Indeed, office work now represents over twice theproportion of GDP that it did in 1955.   This is in sharp contrastto agricultural productivity, where the steam tractor and the combineharvester slashed the proportion ofGDP in agriculture from over 80% to under 5%.  This general lack of real productivity is clearly reflected inmodernlifestyles.  Hard working couples struggle to buy the basic foodand shelter which their grandfathers had purchased while their wivesstayedat home.  If real productivity had risen just 1% pa, it would havealmost doubled since 1955, which means that people could live a1955 lifestyleworking only three days per week.  This is clearly not the case.  To understand why we work harder than our grandparents despite the hugeincrease in productivity we have to examine the effect of productivityin detail.  Consider the following data:-    The key statistic appears in the third line of the table.  The1955 Australian taxation act and regulations was published in twovolumes, consisting of 1,324 pages plus title pages and index. The 2007 act and regulations is over 12 dense volumes consisting ofover 15,698 pages.  We see in the following lines that there hasin fact been a huge improvement in productivity as measured in relationto the size of the act and regulations that are beingadministrated.  It would be quite infeasible to administer thecurrent act with 1955 technology.  A good example of the benefit this extra regulation brings to societyis the Superannuation Co-Contribution scheme which was introduced in2001 to assist the poor to save for their retirement.  It providesthat the government will pay an additional $1,500 into each person'ssuperannuation account provided that they earn less than $28,000, havea lazy $1,000 to pay into their own superannuation account, areemployed (ie. not house wives or students), and know about thescheme.  Thus this marvelous scheme enables the government to be seen tobe generously aiding the poor in a way that very few of the poor cantake advantage of, and thus costing the government very little.  Alarger example is the new Goods and Services (VAT) tax, which isessentially the same as conventional income tax but is governed by a completelydifferent set of regulations.  Thousands of additions havebeen made to the act since 1955, and it is the duty of every Australianto comply with all of them.  Given that the size of a bureaucracy is not related to its function, onemight ask why the size of the tax office has remained between 1% and 2%of GDP for over fifty years, regardless of the technology available toit.  Why not 0.2%, or 35.7%?  The answer is that societycould not tolerate a value much higher than 2% — we would be payingtaxes just to fund the tax collection process.  Below 1% is easilyaffordable, so the bureaucracy will naturally grow beyond that size aspredicted by Parkinson's law.    We now see why it is so critical to society that software projectsfail.  The boundless creativity of politicians and bureaucrats todevelop new and more complex regulation is bounded only by thebureaucracy's inability to implement them.  The absolute size ofthe bureaucracy is constrained by external factors, so the only effectofautomation can be to increase bureaucratic complexity.    It would simply not have been feasible to implement the SuperannuationCo-Contribution scheme in 1955, but automation had made it possible in2001.  Fortunately for society most tax office software projectshave also failed, so the act and regulations have been limited to15,698 pages.  But imagine if all the projects hadsucceeded?  We might need to deal with well over 150,000 pages ofregulations, and society would be in danger of collapsing under theirweight.  Again, one should not assume that these effects are limited togovernment.  In 1955 banking products were very simple. Accounts were maintained, simple and transparent interest calculationswere made, and few options were available.  Accounts today aremuch more complex, with clever options to pay additional fees to haveinterest rates reduced under non-obvious conditions.    A major Australian bank recently undertook a software project tointegrate a credit card loyalty scheme with home loan accounts. This promised customers huge savings provided they fitted exactly intovery narrow and unlikely profiles.  Fortunately the project faileddue to the complexity of integrating disparate software systems, and socustomers were never burdened with the need to understand, evaluate,and manage the scheme.    Another example was the development of a whole of government portalthat the author was directly involved with.  Its aim was tocentralize and automate the issue and payment of government permits andfees, of which over two hundred had been identified.  Fortunatelythe project was a complete failure, at a cost to the tax payer of a fewtens of millions of dollars.    But what a disaster if it had succeeded!  Once the two hundredexisting permits had been automated hundreds more permits andregulations could have been easily created and implementedefficiently.  In this age of "user pays" proposals had ranged froma hair dressing salon operating permit to a pedestrian permit to fundthe construction of foot paths.  Society was only spared thisadditional burden by the failure of the software project.    A naive reader might understand the damage caused by software systems,but might wonder if savings could be made by simply not attempting tobuild them in the first place rather than just having them fail. This of course completely misses the benefit that projects bringdespite their failure to directly produce anything of value. Their development employs many bureaucrats, consultants and contractorsand their expenditure supports an even larger number of peoplethroughout society.  We have provided empirical proof of Berglas's Corollary, and clearlyshown that software does not improve real productivity.  Further,we have shown why it is essential that most software projectsfail.  No one need ever again be embarrassed by participation in afailedsoftware project.  Rather they should be proud to have sparedsocietyfrom yet another burden of complexity.  Some may misinterpret this article as satire.  Surely it is notreally desirable for software projects to fail.  But the factsspeak for themselves. 1941年，冰岛第一批香蕉问世。[1][2]从那时起，它们就开始生产了，每年大约100簇，每簇大约5-20千克(11-4400万磅)，但目前还没有销售。[3]第二次世界大战后，便宜的地热能(最近才有)和进口水果的高价相结合，导致建造了许多温室，从1945年[4]一直到1958年[5]或1959年，香蕉都是在那里商业生产的。[6]1960年，政府取消了水果进口税。[7]国内种植的香蕉不再能与进口香蕉竞争，很快就从市场上消失了。由于日照水平低，冰岛香蕉的生产速度要慢得多；冰岛香蕉需要两年的时间才能成熟，而赤道附近只需要几个月的时间。[8]。
冰岛是欧洲最大的香蕉生产国或出口国的都市神话已经在各种书籍和其他媒体上进行了宣传。在BBC智力竞赛节目QI的一集[9]以及与该节目相关的论坛上都提到了这一点。[10]根据粮农组织的统计数据，欧洲最大的香蕉生产国是法国(在马提尼克岛和瓜德罗普岛)，其次是西班牙[11](主要在加那利群岛)。欧洲的其他香蕉生产国包括葡萄牙(在马德拉)、希腊和意大利。
虽然温室里仍然生长着少量的香蕉植物，每年都会结出果实，但冰岛几乎所有消费的香蕉都是进口的，现在人均每年的进口量超过1800万公斤(4000万磅)。[12]冰岛农业大学在其热带温室中保留了最后一个这样的农场，种植了600-700株香蕉，这些植物在生产者关闭时(当时的园艺学院)作为捐赠接受。那里种植的香蕉供学生和教职员工消费，不出售。[8]。

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  Although  Iceland is reliant upon  fishing,  tourism and  aluminium production as the mainstays of its economy, the production of vegetables and fruit in  greenhouses is a growing sector. Until the 1960s this included commercial production of  bananas.
 In 1941, the first bananas in Iceland were produced.  [1]  [2] They have been produced since that time, about 100 clusters a year each about 5–20 kg (11–44 lb), but are not currently sold.  [3] In the wake of  World War II, the combination of inexpensive  geothermal power (which had recently become available) and high prices for imported fruit led to the construction of a number of greenhouses where bananas were produced commercially from 1945  [4] to as late as 1958  [5] or 1959.  [6] In 1960, the government removed import duties on fruit.  [7] Domestically grown bananas were no longer able to compete with imported ones and soon disappeared from the market. Icelandic banana production was much slower due to low levels of sunlight; Icelandic bananas took two years to mature, while it only takes a few months near the equator.  [8]
 The  urban myth that Iceland is Europe’s largest producer or exporter of bananas has been propagated in various books and other media. It was mentioned, in an episode of the  BBC quiz programme  QI,  [9] and on a forum connected with the show.  [10] According to  FAO statistics, the largest European producer of bananas is France (in  Martinique and  Guadeloupe), followed by  Spain  [11] (primarily in the  Canary Islands). Other banana-producing countries in Europe include  Portugal (on  Madeira),  Greece, and  Italy.
 Although a small number of banana plants still exist in greenhouses and produce fruit every year, Iceland imports nearly all of the bananas consumed in the country, with imports now amounting to over 18 kg (40 lb)  per capita  per annum.  [12] The  Agricultural University of Iceland maintains the last such farm with 600-700 banana plants in its tropical greenhouse, which were received as donations from producers when they shut down (then the   Horticultural College). Bananas grown there are consumed by the students and staff and are not sold.  [8]

note_en:
A  recent thread on nixers got me thinking about the Xserver. We often interract with it either through  startx or a display manager. How many ways can we actually send parameters to the X server, actually how many ways do we have to start an X session.  The first one is through the display manager, this is specific to each one and I'll take the case of lightdm.  lightdm.conf allows to pass parameters to the Xserver via the  xserver-command = directive.  For example:    An obvious way to pass parameters to the Xserver is through its configuration files. For example, Xorg has a bunch in  /etc/X11.  From  man 5 xorg.conf:  SERVERFLAGS SECTION ...        Option "MaxClients"  "integer"               Set the maximum number of clients allowed to connect  to  the  X  server.               Acceptable values are 64, 128, 256 or 512. # so... Section "ServerFlags"        Option "MaxClients"  2048 EndSection
 Now what if you are using startx(1).   NAME        startx - initialize an X session SYNOPSIS        startx [ [ client ] options ... ] [ -- [ server ] [ display ] options ... ]
 Anything after the  -- will be considered arguments to the Xserver, if not starting with  / or  ./  So you can do the following:   startx executes by default the server found in  $HOME/.xserverrc or the one in the xinit directory, usually these days  /etc/X11/xinit/xserverrc.  Similarly, if you are using xinit(1).   NAME        xinit - X Window System initializer SYNOPSIS        xinit [ [ client ] options ... ] [ -- [ server ] [ display ] options ... ]
 Anything after the  -- will be considered arguments to the Xserver, executing by default xserverrc, just like startx.  So why use startx at all, you might ask.  startx is a wrapper over xinit to make it easier to start a session, though...    So what does it do?  It gets the next available display by looping in  /tmp/.X{digit}-lock and  /tmp/.X11-unix/X{digit}, and the tty number by executing  tty(1).  It also sets features for xauth if enabled, like setting the  .Xauthority environment variable, creating an authentication cookie (mcookie) and registering it using  xauth(1). And cleans the allocated vt (deallocvt) and xauth session if needed. But the Xauth isn't mandatory.  In the end you get a line like this to open a session on display  :1 and TTY 2:     So you can definitely pass your arguments to the Xserver there.   But what if we go further and run Xorg ourselves, what do we need xinit for anyway. Let's go all the way.  From the Arch Wiki:  Quote:The Xorg(1) command is usually not run directly, instead the X server is started with either a display manager or xinit.
 It was quite hard to find a way to run the Xorg(1) Xserver directly from the TTY without xinit and without messing up everything. But you can do it in the same way you would launch a Xephyr Xserver or Xvfb Xserver or others — Same o' same o'.  The following starts an Xorg Xserver on display  :1 and TTY 2, and runs the session over it (xinitrc script).   The sleep is sort of mandatory if you want to start Xorg in the background. ( EDIT: or you can  wait for the process like sx does)  So many layers of indirection, startx calls xinit, xinit calls the Xserver.  That's it folks, I hope you enjoy this little escapade. And comment with your own thoughts and ways to start X.
title: 启动Xserver的方法有多种
title_en: Many Ways to Start an Xserver
